did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Machine Learning (ML) for skin cancer detection. Specifically, it employs a novel Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) combined with boosting, along with a Hybrid Color Features (HCF) algorithm for feature extraction from dermoscopy images. The study classifies skin cancers into Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC), achieving accuracies of 95.5%, 96.3%, and 96.7% respectively.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development of a novel Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) and Hybrid Color Features (HCF) algorithm for skin cancer detection. It describes specific methods including image preprocessing (Gaussian noise removal, Otsu segmentation), feature extraction, and model training using 1500 images from the ISIC Archives. The study reports new quantitative results, such as accuracy rates of 95.5%, 96.3%, and 96.7% for Melanoma, BCC, and SCC respectively, and compares these against traditional ML models, indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: Detection of skin cancer through hybrid color features and soft voting ensemble classifier
journal: Innovations in Systems and Software Engineering
brief_summary: This study proposes a machine learning-based computer-aided diagnosis system for classifying skin cancers into Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC) using dermoscopy images from the ISIC Archives. The methodology involves a novel image preprocessing network utilizing Gaussian noise removal, blackhat operations, and Otsu segmentation, followed by feature extraction via a Hybrid Color Features (HCF) algorithm. The core classification model is a Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) combined with boosting, which aggregates predictions from multiple classifiers to enhance accuracy. The proposed approach achieved accuracies of 95.5%, 96.3%, and 96.7% for Melanoma, BCC, and SCC, respectively. These results demonstrate that the SVEC approach outperforms traditional machine learning models and prior initiatives in detecting skin cancer types.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the three primary skin cancer types classified in the study, with 500 images used from the ISIC Archives. Achieved 95.5% accuracy.

all_task_types.target: Skin cancer type (Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Squamous Cell Carcinoma)
all_task_types.details: The paper focuses on classifying skin cancer into three discrete diagnostic categories: Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC). The study employs a Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) to predict these classes based on hybrid color features extracted from dermoscopy images. The primary task is multiclass classification, with reported accuracies of 95.5%, 96.3%, and 96.7% for the respective classes.

datasets_used.name: ISIC Archives
datasets_used.description: An open-access, free platform for skin cancer research providing dermoscopy images of various skin cancer types. The study utilized images from this source, which were preprocessed to remove noise and artifacts.

use.training: Used for training the Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) and other baseline classifiers.
use.testing: Used for evaluating the performance of the proposed model and comparing it with other classifiers.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Outlier handling and feature normalization/standardization to remove mean and scale data to unit variance.
Feature normalization to scale each feature to a given range (default [0,1]).
Feature normalization to remove the median and scale data according to the quantile range (IQR).
Classification by combining predictions of multiple models (SVM, Logistic Regression, Decision Tree, K-Nearest Neighbor) using weighted votes proportional to their probability of being correct.

Assigning weights to individual classifiers (SVM: 0.5, Logistic Regression: 1.0, Decision Tree: 1.5, K-Nearest Neighbor: 2.0) based on their relative performance/accuracy to improve overall classification accuracy.
Determining the primary classifier and assigning weights by comparing the accuracy of each classifier on a holdout set.
Dividing the dataset into training and testing sets to ensure adequate model training and evaluation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score, with comparisons made based on these metric values rather than statistical significance.

ISIC Archives.dataset_description: Total 1500 images (500 Melanoma, 500 BCC, 500 SCC) from ISIC Archives.

baseline_individual_classifiers.description: Accuracy of individual classifiers on the holdout set used for weight determination.

ensemble_classifiers_comparison.description: Average Accuracy and F1-scores for various classifiers on the full dataset.

proposed_method_per_class_results.description: Accuracy, Precision, Recall, and F1-score for the proposed Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) with Hybrid Color Features (HCF) on each specific class.

ISIC Archives.notes: The proposed SVEC with boosting achieved accuracies of 95.5%, 96.3%, and 96.7% for Melanoma, BCC, and SCC respectively. The combined preprocessed feature set increased the accuracy of individual models by 7-9%. Gradient Boosting achieved 91% accuracy in some contexts mentioned, but Table 3 lists it at 91.5% Avg Accuracy. The SVEC outperformed all listed baselines.

architecture_used.main_model: Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) with boosting

architecture_details.feature_extraction: Hybrid Color Features (HCF) algorithm (texture, shape, and color features)

training_details.dataset: ISIC Archives (1500 images: 500 Melanoma, 500 BCC, 500 SCC)
training_details.optimization: Hyper-tuning during test method to optimize weights based on recall and precision

classification_or_output_head.mechanism: Assigns probability to each class and predicts the type with the highest probability; weights are proportional to the probability of the voter's vote being correct

preprocessing_techniques: Gaussian noise removal (using median blur filter)
Hair/artifact removal (using dull razor (DR) algorithm and blackhat morphological operator)
conclusions: The proposed Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) combined with boosting and Hybrid Color Features (HCF) outperforms traditional machine learning models and prior initiatives in detecting skin cancer types.
The SVEC approach achieved classification accuracies of 95.5% for Melanoma, 96.3% for Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC), and 96.7% for Squamous Cell Carcinoma (SCC).
The novel image preprocessing network, comprising Gaussian noise removal, blackhat operations, and Otsu segmentation, effectively cleans dermoscopy images prior to feature extraction.
The Hybrid Color Features (HCF) algorithm successfully extracts texture, shape, and color features, which are critical for distinguishing between different skin cancer types.
The combined preprocessed feature set increased the accuracy of individual baseline models by 7–9%.
The study confirms the novelty and effectiveness of the SVEC approach through peer comparison with existing machine learning initiatives.
important_remarks: The study relies on a small, balanced dataset of only 1500 images (500 per class) from the ISIC Archives, which limits the generalizability of the results to broader, real-world clinical populations.
No external validation was performed; the model was evaluated solely on the internal holdout set from the ISIC Archives, raising concerns about overfitting to this specific dataset's distribution.
The paper does not report formal statistical significance testing (e.g., p-values) for the performance improvements, relying instead on direct metric comparisons.

The Soft Voting Ensemble Classifier (SVEC) weights were determined using a holdout set evaluation, which may introduce data leakage if the holdout set was not strictly separated from the training process or if the weights were tuned on the test data.

The preprocessing pipeline includes complex steps like the dull razor algorithm for hair removal and Otsu segmentation, which may fail or introduce artifacts on images with dense hair or poor contrast, potentially affecting robustness.
The study focuses on three specific skin cancer types (Melanoma, BCC, SCC) and does not address benign lesions or other malignancies, limiting its clinical utility as a comprehensive diagnostic tool.
The reported accuracies (95.5%-96.7%) are high but may be optimistic due to the lack of cross-validation across multiple folds or independent datasets.
